1: haṭṭēh YHWH ʾoznĕkhā ʿănēnî — turn your ear toward me, O LORD, and answer me. The hiphil of nāṭāh (to incline, stretch) applied to God's ear — the physical gesture of one leaning in to hear. kî-ʿānî wĕʾebyôn ʾānî — for I am poor and needy. The double-term formula ʿānî wĕʾebyôn (poor and destitute) recurs throughout the Psalter (Ps 40:17; 70:5; 109:22) as the basis for divine aid — absolute need as the only qualification for prayer.
2: shĕmor nafshî kî-ḥāsîd ʾānî — preserve my soul, for I am devoted to you. Ḥāsîd (faithful, covenant-loyal, pious — from ḥesed: the one who embodies covenant love) — the Psalmist's self-identification as a ḥāsîd is both a claim and an appeal: God's ḥesed should protect the one who embodies ḥesed. hôshaʿ ʿabdĕkhā ʾattāh ʾĕlōhay habboṭēaḥ ʾēlêkhā — save your servant who trusts in you, O my God. Three self-identifications (devoted, servant, trusting) build the case for divine response.
3: ḥonnēnî ʾădōnāy kî ʾēlêkhā ʾeqrāʾ kol-hayyôm — be gracious to me, O Lord, for I cry out to you all day long. Ḥānan (to be gracious — the divine attribute of Exod 34:6 that recurs in v15). The frequency of prayer (kol-hayyôm: all day long) is itself an argument for a response — persistent prayer makes an implicit claim on divine attentiveness. Psalm 86 is notably constructed from phrases borrowed from many other psalms; v3 echoes Ps 56:1; 57:1.
4: śammēaḥ nefesh ʿabdekā — gladden the soul of your servant. Śāmaḥ (hiphil: to cause to rejoice, to make glad) — the petition is not just for rescue but for joy: full salvation includes gladness, not merely safety. kî ʾēlêkhā ʾădōnāy nafshî ʾeśśā — for to you, O Lord, I lift up my soul. Lifting the soul (nefesh) to God — the gesture of complete surrender and reorientation of one's center of gravity toward God (cf. Ps 25:1; 143:8).
5: kî-ʾattāh ʾădōnāy ṭôb wĕsallāḥ — for you, O Lord, are good and forgiving. Sallāḥ (forgiving — a qal adjective from sālaḥ: to forgive) occurs only here in the entire OT as an attribute of God — everywhere else sālaḥ is a verb. The nominal form makes forgiveness a permanent divine characteristic, not just an occasional divine act. wĕrab-ḥesed lĕkhol-qōrĕʾêkhā — abounding in steadfast love for all who call on you. Rab-ḥesed (great in steadfast love) — the phrase from Exod 34:6 (wĕrab-ḥesed wĕʾemet) cited directly. All who call — not just Israel.
6: haʾăzînāh YHWH tĕphillātî — give ear to my prayer, O LORD. The hiphil of ʾāzan (to give ear) — a second request for divine attentiveness, completing the frame opened in v1. wĕhaqshîbāh bĕqôl taḥănûnôtāy — listen to the voice of my plea for mercy. Taḥănûn (plea for grace/mercy — from ḥānan: to be gracious) — the prayer as an appeal to divine grace, not a claim of divine obligation.
7: bĕyôm ṣārātî ʾeqrāʾekā — on the day of my trouble I call to you. The connection between crisis and prayer: the day of trouble (ṣārāh) is the day of calling. kî taʿănēnî — for you answer me. The certainty is grammatically present tense — not a hope but a known fact drawn from experience. The prayer is made precisely because the answer is certain, not speculatively.
8: ʾên-kāmôkhā bāʾĕlōhîm ʾădōnāy — there is no one like you among the gods, O Lord. The incomparability formula (ên kāmôkhā) acknowledges the existence of other divine claimants while asserting their absolute inferiority to YHWH (cf. Exod 15:11; Ps 35:10; 89:6). wĕʾên kĕmaʿăśêkhā — and no works compare to yours. The incomparability extends to deeds, not just being — YHWH's actions are uniquely powerful.
9: kol-gôyim ʾăsher ʿāśîtā yābôʾû wĕyishtaḥăwû lĕphānêkhā ʾădōnāy — all the nations you have made will come and bow before you, O Lord. The petition moves from personal crisis to eschatological vision: all nations — precisely because YHWH made them (ʾăsher ʿāśîtā) — will ultimately worship their Creator. wîkhabbĕdû lishmekhā — and they will glorify your name. The universal worship envisioned in Ps 72:11; 87:4-6 surfaces here as grounds for present prayer.
10: kî-gādôl ʾattāh wĕʿōśēh niplāʾôt — for you are great and you do wonders. Niplāʾôt (wonders — the Exodus vocabulary of Ps 77:14; 78:4) — the divine greatness expressed in specific historical acts. ʾattāh ʾĕlōhîm lĕbaddekā — you alone are God. Lĕbaddekā (you alone — the exclusive particle) — a direct monotheistic confession, not merely incomparability but uniqueness. No other being is ʾĕlōhîm in the same sense.
11: hôrēnî YHWH darkekā ʾăhalēkh baʾămittekhā — teach me your way, O LORD, that I may walk in your truth. Hôrēnî (hiphil imperative of yārāh: to teach, direct — the same root as tôrāh) — the request is for ongoing guidance in the divine path, not merely information. yaḥēd lĕbābî lĕyirʾāh shĕmekā — give me an undivided heart to reverence your name. Yāḥad lēbāb (to make the heart one, undivided) — hapax in this form; the undivided heart is the antithesis of double-mindedness (Jas 1:8). The Shema's call to love with the whole heart (Deut 6:5) as a personal petition.
12: ʾôdekhā ʾădōnāy ʾĕlōhay bĕkhol-lĕbābî — I will thank you, O Lord my God, with my whole heart. Bĕkhol-lĕbābî (with all my heart) — the fulfillment of the petition of v11 (undivided heart): the whole heart now offered in praise. waʾăkhabĕdāh shimĕkhā lĕʿôlām — I will glorify your name forever. The temporal scope extends the personal praise to eternity — the individual's commitment is to permanent, unceasing glorification.
13: kî-ḥasdĕkhā gādôl ʿālay — for your steadfast love toward me is great. Gādôl ʿālay (great over/toward me) — the divine ḥesed measured not in abstract but in its personal weight, felt as a presence. wĕhiṣṣaltā nafshî mishshĕʾôl taḥtiyyāh — you have rescued my soul from the lowest depths of Sheol. Taḥtiyyāh (lowest, undermost — the superlative of taḥtî: the lowest level of Sheol) — the deepest pit of the underworld. The rescue is past (perfect: hiṣṣaltā) — the Psalmist testifies to a completed deliverance.
14: ʾĕlōhîm zēdîm qāmû-ʿālay — O God, arrogant men have risen up against me. Zēdîm (arrogant ones, presumptuous — those who act with overweening pride against God and neighbor). waʿădat ʿārîṣîm biqqĕshû nafshî — a band of ruthless men seeks my life. ʿĀrîṣ (violent, ruthless, a tyrant). wĕlōʾ śāmûkhā lĕnegdām — they do not keep you before them. The defining sin of the adversary: God is not lĕnegdām (before/opposite them) — they live as practical atheists, with no divine reference point (cf. Ps 10:4; 36:1).
15: wĕʾattāh ʾădōnāy ʾēl-raḥûm wĕḥannûn — but you, O Lord, are a compassionate and gracious God. The direct citation of Exod 34:6 — the Mosaic self-revelation of YHWH's character at Sinai. The full formula there is raḥûm wĕḥannûn ʾerek ʾappayim wĕrab-ḥesed wĕʾemet (compassionate and gracious, slow to anger and abounding in steadfast love and faithfulness). Ps 86:5 cited ṭôb wĕsallāḥ wĕrab-ḥesed; now v15 cites the core attributes directly. This Exodus 34 formula is the most-cited divine self-description in the OT (Num 14:18; Neh 9:17; Ps 103:8; 145:8; Joel 2:13; Jon 4:2).
16: pĕnēh ʾēlay wĕḥonnēnî — turn to me and be gracious to me. The second round of petitions: pānāh (to turn toward — the opposite of the divine hiding-of-face) + ḥānan (to be gracious). tĕnāh ʿuzzĕkhā lĕʿabdekā — give your strength to your servant. ʿŌz (strength, power) as the divine gift: the Psalmist does not already possess what is needed; it must be given. wĕhôshîʿāh lĕben-ʾămātekhā — and save the son of your maidservant. Ben-ʾāmāh (son of the maidservant) — the idiom for the household-born slave, fully belonging to the household from birth. The self-description claims a hereditary membership in YHWH's household.
17: ʿăśēh-ʿimmî ʾôt lĕṭôbāh — show me a sign of your favor. ʾôt (sign — a visible marker of divine intention or presence, as in the Exodus signs) requested as confirmation of favor (ṭôbāh: goodness). wĕyirʾû śōnĕʾay wĕyēbōshû — so that those who hate me will see and be ashamed. The enemies' shame is the social reversal that confirms divine favor — not vengeance for its own sake but vindication that makes God's commitment publicly visible. kî-ʾattāh YHWH ʿăzartanî wĕniḥamtanî — because you, O LORD, have helped me and comforted me. The psalm closes with past-tense certainty: the help is already received in the confidence of faith.
